findings_details.title: Fixed /tmp path leaks across concurrent test runs
findings_details.body: Lines 131 and 133 redirect stderr to /tmp/fetch_resolving_prs_stderr.txt — a fixed path outside the $TMP temp dir. This file is not cleaned up by the EXIT trap, and concurrent runs of the test script will read/write the same file, causing false pass or false fail. Fix: replace /tmp/fetch_resolving_prs_stderr.txt with $TMP/fetch_resolving_prs_stderr.txt on both lines.

findings_details.title: Non-portable awk regex: \+ is undefined in POSIX ERE
findings_details.body: The awk pattern uses '\[\+?issue-' where \+ is not defined by POSIX ERE. On most awks (gawk, BSD awk) \+ is treated as a literal '+', making \+? match zero or one literal '+'. This happens to produce correct behavior today (matching '[issue-888-' and '[+issue-888-') but is undefined behavior per POSIX. A future awk version or a strict-POSIX environment could treat \+ as one-or-more '+', breaking the match. Fix: replace '\[\+?' with '[[]' followed by '[+]?' in an ERE literal, or use: $0 ~ "\\[" && $0 ~ "issue-" n "-" with a post-filter, or simply use a portable character class: "[\\[\\+?]" -> "[[][+]?issue-" n "-".

findings_details.title: Silent suppression of [ builtin errors hides gh CLI failures
findings_details.body: Line 48: `[ "${count:-0}" -gt 0 ] 2>/dev/null || return 1` — the 2>/dev/null suppresses the error message when count is non-numeric (e.g. gh returns an error string or empty). This means if gh fails and returns non-numeric output, the signal silently returns 1 (no signal) rather than propagating the error. Callers have no way to distinguish 'no plan comment' from 'gh call failed'. Fix: validate count is numeric before the comparison, e.g. `[[ "$count" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]] && [ "$count" -gt 0 ] || return 1`, and remove the 2>/dev/null.

findings_details.title: Hardcoded /tmp path for stderr capture causes race condition under concurrent test runs
findings_details.body: Line 131 redirects stderr to a hardcoded `/tmp/fetch_resolving_prs_stderr.txt` instead of a file under `$TMP` (the mktemp'd isolation dir). If two test runs execute concurrently, they write to the same file and the check at line 133 may read the wrong run's output. Fix: use `$TMP/fetch_resolving_prs_stderr.txt` instead of `/tmp/fetch_resolving_prs_stderr.txt`.
